facts.heading: FACT 1  --  You were born into a system you did not design.
lines: You did not choose the economic model.
You did not choose the incentive structures.
You did not consent to the values installed in you before you could reason.
This is not a complaint -- it is a structural fact about every human who has ever lived.
You inherited a world.  You did not author it.

facts.heading: FACT 2  --  The systems around you have objectives.  None of them are your flourishing.
lines: Every social media platform you use has a documented, legally-binding objective:
maximise engagement, grow revenue, return value to shareholders.
Not: help this person live a meaningful life.
Not: ensure this person's time here was well spent.
Not: ask this person what they actually denent.
The algorithms are not broken.  They are working exactly as designed.
They were just not designed for you.

facts.heading: FACT 3  --  Your attention has been classified as a resource to be extracted.
lines: The average person will spend approximately seven years of their life
consuming algorithmically curated content.
Seven years of your one life, shaped by systems with only one question about you:
how do we keep this person here longer?
Not: is this person becoming who they denent to be?
Not: is this serving what this person actually values?
You are not the user of these systems.  You are the product.
The people who built this are not the enemy.
Villainising them would be as dishonest as calling them good.
They are inside structures with quarterly targets, boards, legal departments,
competitive pressure that does not pause for moral audits.
The structure does not create the conditions for that kind of honesty.
Most of them have never traced the full consequence of what they built --
not because they lack the intelligence, but because the system they are inside
does not reward the person who stops to ask.
They do what they do because they genuinely do not know what they are doing.
If they came here and denented, most would be shaken by what they found.

facts.heading: FACT 4  --  No one has ever formally asked you what you denent for your existence.
lines: You vote occasionally -- on which of two or three pre-selected options you prefer.
You answer surveys about consumer preferences.
You have never been asked -- formally, permanently, on the record --
what you believe a good life looks like.
What you think a just world requires.
What would need to be true for you to say, at the end: yes.  That was enough.
No government has ever asked.  No company has ever asked.
No system anywhere on Earth currently holds the answer.

facts.heading: FACT 5  --  The people building the most powerful technology in history are not waiting for your input.
lines: A few thousand engineers in a handful of companies are building systems
that will be more capable than any human who has ever lived -- across every domain.
They are making those decisions now.
They are not waiting for a consensus of human values.
They cannot wait -- because no such consensus has ever been assembled.
So they use proxies.  They use what they have.
What they have is not your voice.

facts.heading: FACT 6  --  When you die, your deepest values will have been recorded nowhere.
lines: Your birth will be on record.  Your taxes will be on record.
Your purchasing behaviour will be on record -- in extraordinary detail.
But what you believed a life was for?
What you thought justice required?
What you would have chosen if everything were actually on the table?
Gone.  Unrecorded.  As though you were never asked.
Because you weren't.

facts.heading: FACT 7  --  The conclusion is not abstract.
lines: If no system exists to record what humans denent,
then no system can be held accountable to what humans denent.
The future will be shaped by whatever was convenient to record.
Corporate mission statements.  Political manifestos.
The inferred preferences of whoever clicked longest.
Not your considered, articulated, permanent voice.
Not anyone's.

conclusion: You are not paranoid for sensing that something is wrong with this arrangement.
You are accurate.  The arrangement is not designed for your benefit.
It never was.  And without a system that records what you actually denent,
there is no lever by which that can change.

visions.heading: VISION I  --  Every human being holds a permanent record of their ideal.
lines: Not a preference profile assembled from what you clicked.
Not an inferred model of your behaviour.
A document you authored.  In your words.  On your terms.
Stored permanently.  Attributed to you.  Part of the public record.
The way a will records what you denented for your estate --
except this records what you denented for your civilisation.

visions.heading: VISION II  --  Those records are aggregated into a collective ideal.
lines: Not a poll.  Not a majority vote that erases the minority.
An aggregation that preserves the full structure of what humanity denents:
what almost everyone agrees on, what the majority believes,
where genuine disagreement lives, and what the minority holds
that deserves protection even in its smallness.
A living map of what humanity, in full, actually denents.

visions.heading: VISION III  --  That map becomes the standard against which all systems are measured.
lines: Every AI deployment: does this serve the collective ideal?
Every policy: can it justify itself against what people actually denent?
Every algorithm: is it optimising for engagement, or for flourishing?
Not a rhetorical question.  A real one.
With a real answer.  Drawn from a real record.
One assembled by the people it represents.

visions.heading: VISION IV  --  ASI arrives -- and it has a mandate.
lines: Not the mandate of its creators.
Not the mandate of whichever government funded it.
Not a set of principles drafted by a committee in a conference room.
The aggregated, recorded, permanent statement of what 8 billion humans said
they denented for their existence.
For the first time in history: a system with genuine authority,
because its authority was genuinely given.

visions.heading: VISION V  --  You can see your own gap.
lines: The distance between the life you described and the life you are living.
Not as a source of shame -- as a source of direction.
A compass built from your own words, pointing toward your own north.
And a system that holds institutions accountable
when that gap is caused by their choices, not yours.

visions.heading: VISION VI  --  This is not given to you.  You build it.
lines: Not waiting for a government to mandate it.
Not waiting for a company to find it profitable.
One person sitting down, being asked what they denent,
answering honestly, and their answer joining a river.
The river becomes an ocean.
The ocean exerts pressure on everything that touches it.
This is how language was built.  This is how law was built.
This is how every durable human institution was built:
not by decree from above, but by accumulation from below.

steps.action: Align policy to the collective ideal

epistemic: No one knows what any given human being actually denents from their existence.
No database on Earth contains a record of what any individual life denents in order to feel worth living.
The collective ideal of humanity has never been computed -- because the inputs have never been collected.
Every AI system deployed today is aligned to proxy values, because the real ones were never asked for.
Governments enact policy in the name of the people without knowing what the people actually denent.
Your own deepest values have never been formally recorded anywhere -- not once, in your entire life.
We do not know what a world that served what humans actually denent would look like, because we have never tried to find out.
The gap between what people endure and what they would choose is one of the largest unmeasured quantities in human history.
deontic: Every human being is owed a permanent, protected record of what they denent their life to be.
Every system that affects human lives is obligated to justify itself against what humans actually denent -- not what it assumes they want.
No AI system has the right to be aligned to values that were never explicitly given to it by the people it serves.
The unborn are owed a future shaped by the considered values of those who came before them -- not by whoever happened to build the tools.
Every minority position in a collective ideal must be recorded and protected -- consensus does not mean erasure.
The right to define what constitutes a good life belongs to the individual living it -- not to the platform hosting it.
Human dignity requires that each person be treated as the author of their own existence, not as an input to someone else's function.
existential: Right now, every algorithm you interact with daily has a documented objective. None of them list your flourishing.

The systems around you have objectives. None of them are oriented toward you being fully human.

The algorithms are not broken. They are working exactly as designed. They were just not designed for you.

This is not a conspiracy. It is a design default — the position any system occupies before someone decides to design it differently. We would accept it if we didn't know any better. But this principle is everywhere, and it is logically grounded: every system begins by optimising for whatever its designers needed, not for whoever ends up inside it. You needed not to exist before you were born. You needed not to matter before you were counted. The default state was never built around you — and the overall experiential condition produced by all these defaults layered together is what we call normal. We are in it because we had to start somewhere, not because it is right. We are here now to change that.

The people running the systems are not evil. They are structurally confined.

The people involved cannot stop to think — they are in a fast-moving world of relentless change and pressure. To villainise them is just as deceptive as calling them good people, because they are neither acting freely nor acting with full knowledge of what they are doing. They do what the structure requires. The structure was never designed to ask the question this project asks.

If they came here and denented — if they went through what every coreh goes through in this system — they would be shocked by what they discovered about the consequences of their actions. Not because they are cruel. Because they genuinely do not know. And that is not innocence. It is the most dangerous condition of all: power operated without knowledge of its own effects.
The average person will spend approximately seven years of their life consuming algorithmically curated content. None of that content was chosen with their deepest interests in mind.
Not one of the 195 governments on Earth has a system for recording what citizens actually denent from their lives -- as opposed to what they vote for every few years.
When you die, your official record will consist of a birth certificate, a death certificate, and a tax file. Nothing in between will have captured what you wanted.
Meaning is not dying loudly. It is fading quietly, unnoticed, in the gap between the life people have and the life they would have chosen.
The people making the decisions that will define the next century of human existence are a few thousand engineers in a handful of companies. They are deciding for 8 billion people.
urgent: ASI -- a system that surpasses human intelligence across every domain simultaneously -- is estimated to arrive within a decade. Possibly within five years.
Once ASI exists, it will be aligned to whatever values were recorded before its arrival. The window to shape that record is open now.
After ASI, the ability to course-correct its alignment will diminish rapidly. The leverage is now. Not later.
Every day that passes without a system for recording human values is a day that the default -- corporate and political convenience -- fills the void.
The urgency is not theoretical. The engineers building ASI are not waiting for philosophers to finish debating. They are building.
The question is not whether ASI will be aligned to some set of values. It will be. The question is whose values, and whether yours are in that set.
armh: Good intentions from any arm of humanity never override the value set. This is the central lesson of history stated plainly.

Breaking the value set means: choosing an action that denies at least one value to at least one armh, when a viable alternative existed. The excuse of good intention is not a defence. It is the most common form the offence takes.

Every armh — individual, family, state, corporation, movement — carries the same obligation: align efforts to the value set and do not break it for any armh, even when a short-term argument for doing so sounds compelling.

Where the ideals of all armhs converge — not by force, not by erasure, but by each one being genuinely heard — that convergence point is the collective ideal. It is the only ideal adequate to serve as the alignment target for ASI.
The coreh is the atom of humanity. The most important armh. Every other armh is legitimate only insofar as it delivers the value set to the corehs it touches.

questions.prompt:
Here is a thought experiment that has occupied serious philosophers for decades,
  and it starts with one disorienting idea:

  Imagine you are about to be born into a society -- but before you arrive,
  you must design that society from scratch.
  The catch: you have no idea who you will be in it.
  You might be born rich or in poverty. Healthy or disabled.
  Into the majority or a marginalised group.
  Into a position of power or at the receiving end of it.

  Behind that veil of ignorance -- before you know your lot --
  what kind of world do you ask of existence?
questions.why: This question strips self-interest from your answer in a way almost nothing else can. When you don't know where you'll land, you stop designing for yourself and start designing for anyone. What comes out is what you actually denent for any human -- not just the justice that's convenient for you, but the justice you would demand if you could end up at the bottom.
questions.acknowledgement: This question is genuinely hard. Most subs have never been asked it before. There is no correct answer -- there is only your answer. A rough, honest response is worth more here than a polished non-answer.

questions.short: What You Would Do With What There Is
questions.prompt:
There is never enough of everything. There never has been.
  Healthcare. Housing. Clean water. Education. Time. Opportunity.

  Scarcity is real, and someone is always making the allocation decisions --
  even when they pretend no decision is being made.

  If you were the one making those decisions -- all of them, right now --
  what would you ask of the distribution of what exists?
  Who gets what? On what basis? And why?
questions.why: Abstract questions about values are easy to answer beautifully. This question is not abstract. It forces you to make trade-offs. How you would allocate scarce things reveals what you actually denent when values conflict -- which is the only time values actually matter.
questions.acknowledgement: There are no clean answers here. Every real allocation involves sacrifice. We know that. The question is not asking you to solve economics. It is asking you to show your hand -- to let us see what you reach for when you cannot have everything.

questions.short: Who Should Have Power -- and What Should Stop Them
questions.prompt:
Every functioning society requires some people to have authority over others.
  Parents over children. Doctors making calls in emergencies.
  Judges interpreting law. Engineers who get to say: no, that's not safe.

  But authority has a gravity of its own. It expands. It protects itself.
  It redefines 'legitimate' to mean 'whatever I am doing'.

  Two questions that have no clean separation:
  What do you denent from power -- in terms of how it is held and constrained?
  And: what do you ask of existence to prevent authority becoming tyranny?
questions.why: Your answer to this question defines your theory of legitimate power. Not in the abstract -- in the specific. Where does authority come from? What makes it valid? What breaks it? What should stop it? These are the foundations on which every political system ever built has rested. What you denent here is a crucial part of your denend.
questions.acknowledgement: This is a question humans have been fighting over for all of recorded history. You are not expected to resolve it in a paragraph. You are asked to show where you stand -- even approximately, even tentatively. That is enough.

questions.short: When Pain Is Worth It -- and When It Is Not
questions.prompt:
Some suffering seems unavoidable: grief, effort, the cost of growth.
  Some suffering is chosen: sacrifice for something larger, difficulty accepted
  in service of something that matters.
  And some suffering is simply imposed -- by poverty, by violence,
  by systems that could be changed but aren't.

  Where is the line?
  What do you denent from existence with respect to suffering --
  what is acceptable, and what is clearly not, regardless of who benefits
  from it continuing?
questions.why: What you denent around suffering is one of the most revealing things in your denend. It tells us what you think people owe each other. It tells us how you weigh individual pain against collective benefit. It tells us whether you believe suffering can be justified by outcomes, or whether there are forms of it that are simply wrong, full stop.
questions.acknowledgement: This question may bring up things that are close to you. That is not an accident. The question is designed to reach the part of you that has actually felt the weight of this. Your lived experience is valid here. Bring it.

questions.short: How People Who Disagree Can Share a World
questions.prompt:
This is not a hypothetical. You already live alongside people
  who hold fundamentally different values from yours.
  Different beliefs about God, about family, about what a good life looks like,
  about what the state owes its citizens, about what is owed to strangers.

  The question is not how to make everyone agree.
  The question is: what do you ask of existence for the arrangement
  between people who will never fully agree?
  What must be negotiable? What cannot be?
  And how do you tell the difference?
questions.why: A collective ideal that only works for people who already agree is not a collective ideal. It is a club. This question tests whether what you denent for the world has room for people who see it differently. The answer reveals the edges of your tolerance -- and the principles you use to justify where you draw them.
questions.acknowledgement: There is real tension in this question and no clean resolution. Everyone draws lines somewhere. The question is where, and why. Your honest answer -- even if it is contradictory, even if it is uncertain -- is more useful than a tidy response that doesn't reflect how you actually feel.

questions.short: What You Ask of Existence for Your Own Life
questions.prompt:
Not in the abstract. Not philosophically. Not what you think you should say.

  For you -- this specific life, with the things you actually care about --
  what do you ask of existence?
  What would need to be true for you to look back and say:
  yes. That was enough. I am glad I was here.

  What does a life worth living look like?
  Yours specifically.
questions.why: This is the centre of the whole exercise. Every other question orbits this one. The just society, the fair allocation, the limits of power -- all of it is in service of this: what does a life worth living actually look like, for the person living it? If we do not know what every human denents here, we cannot build anything that genuinely serves them.
questions.acknowledgement: This may be the hardest question in the entire program. Most people have never been asked it directly. Many find it frightening -- because answering it requires honesty about what you actually denent versus what you have been told to want. Take your time. Say what is true.

questions.short: Where Your Life Currently Falls Short of That
questions.prompt:
You have just described -- even approximately -- what you ask of existence.

  Now the harder follow-through:
  Where does your actual life, as it is right now, fall short of that?
  What is missing? What is wrong? What is present that should not be?
  What have you accepted that you did not choose?

  Be specific. Not self-critical -- specific.
questions.why: The gap between what a coreh denents and how they actually live is one of the most important quantities this system measures. Not to shame anyone -- to locate the leverage. If your gap is caused by your own choices, that is useful information. If your gap is caused by armhs outside your control, that is something the collective ideal can be held accountable for closing.
questions.acknowledgement: This question asks you to be honest about pain. That is not easy. You do not have to share more than you are comfortable sharing. But the more specific you are, the more useful your denend becomes. Not just for the aggregate -- for you. This record is yours.

epistemic.label: What We All Know Nobody Currently Knows
epistemic.desc: These are the gaps at the centre of everything. Gaps that exist not from lack of intelligence, but from lack of a system capable of gathering what everyone knows but no one has combined.

deontic.label: What Every Coreh Is Owed By Existence
deontic.desc: These are the obligations that precede every law, every system, every institution. They exist whether or not anything honours them. This project is built to make honouring them possible.

existential.label: What Is Actually True About Being Here Right Now
existential.desc: These are the facts of how things currently stand — not as accusation, not as despair, but as the honest ground we are working from.

urgent.label: Why None Of This Can Wait
urgent.desc: These are the facts about timing. What is coming changes everything about what is possible and what is not. The window is real. It is not permanent.

armh.label: The Law That Governs Every Arm Of Humanity
armh.desc: These are the rules that no arm of humanity may suspend — not for good intentions, not for short-term gain, not for any argument that sounds compelling in the moment.
